critic_raw:
The multi-modal immunotherapy response prediction experiment has several commendable design choices: stratified random split with fixed seed, BioBERT encoder not retrained on the cohort (preventing leakage), preprocessing fit on training only, matched-architecture baseline (genomic-only variant), bootstrap CIs on test metrics, and subgroup analyses. However, important concerns remain.

First, the sample size of 500 patients with a ~30-40% response rate (approximately 100-150 positive cases in the test set of ~100 patients: 50 positives at best) is severely underpowered for a test set evaluation of AUROC. With ~50 positive cases in a test set of 100, bootstrap confidence intervals on AUROC will span approximately 0.10-0.15. This means the comparison between multi-modal and genomic-only models will almost certainly not be statistically distinguishable on the test set alone. The paper needs to acknowledge this explicitly and justify why the test set results would be interpretable.

Second, the stratified random split (not temporal) is justified by 'treatment response is a fixed post-hoc outcome.' However, in a retrospective cohort, earlier patients may have received different treatment protocols, different tumor profiling technology, and less complete genomic data compared to later patients. A random split assigns patients from different treatment eras to training and test, potentially mixing different data quality regimes and introducing treatment era as an unmeasured confounder. A temporal split with a sensitivity analysis would better characterize temporal confounding.

Third, the multi-modal model has three branches (genomic, tabular clinical, text) while the baseline uses only the genomic branch. The clinical tabular and text branches add model parameters and regularization components. Even with 'identical architecture but genomic-only,' the multi-modal model has substantially more parameters. The defense concedes in the design that both models 'use the same learning rate schedule' — but matched learning rates do not prevent capacity differences from influencing convergence and overfitting differently.
all_issues_raised: Test set of ~100 patients (~50 positives) too small for statistically distinguishable AUROC comparison
Stratified random split may mix treatment eras with different protocols and genomic data quality — temporal split warranted
Multi-modal model has more parameters than genomic-only baseline despite 'identical architecture' claim; capacity mismatch affects regularization

round_transcripts.critic: The design is careful in many respects: matched-architecture baseline, BioBERT not fine-tuned, preprocessing split-safe, bootstrap CIs specified. Three concerns remain: (1) test set of ~100 patients (~50 positives) yields bootstrap AUROC CIs spanning 0.10-0.15 — comparison will be statistically indistinguishable; (2) random split may confound treatment eras and protocol changes over the 5-year cohort; (3) multi-modal model has more parameters than genomic-only baseline despite architectural matching claim.
round_transcripts.defender:
On test set power: this is a genuine constraint of a 500-patient cohort. Bootstrap CIs are specified in the design, which will expose the wide uncertainty. The paper can present the results as directional evidence with appropriately hedged conclusions rather than claiming statistical significance. Alternatively, cross-validation with all 500 patients would provide narrower confidence intervals at the cost of a formal held-out test.

On random vs. temporal split: the design justifies random split because treatment response is assigned prospectively per RECIST at 12 weeks. However, the critic is correct that earlier patients may have had different genomic profiling technology (earlier generation sequencing panels have lower sensitivity for certain mutations) and different treatment protocols (e.g., combination immunotherapy versus monotherapy). A temporal analysis comparing model performance on earlier versus later cohort years would be informative.

On capacity mismatch: the design states 'identical architecture but genomic-only' — meaning the multi-modal model has three branches and the baseline has one, with a shared fusion layer. This is not identical capacity: the multi-modal model has significantly more parameters. Learning rate parity does not control for parameter count. The design should either (a) reduce the multi-modal model to match parameter count, or (b) use dropout/weight decay to match effective capacity, or (c) acknowledge this as a limitation.

round_transcripts.critic: The defense addresses the power concern by proposing cross-validation or hedged conclusions — both valid mitigations, but the design as written uses a held-out test set. Whether cross-validation is used is unconfirmed. The temporal confounding issue receives a partial defense but no confirmation of sensitivity analysis. The capacity mismatch is conceded directly. All three issues remain at least partially open.
round_transcripts.defender: On capacity: this is a genuine design gap. The multi-modal model has more parameters than the genomic-only baseline. The current design does not control for this. On power: cross-validation is proposed but not confirmed in the described design. On temporal confounding: a sensitivity analysis is proposed but not described as part of the experiment. All three issues are real and remain in the described design.

round_transcripts.critic: Three issues remain unresolved after two rounds: underpowered test set, potential temporal confounding from random split, and capacity mismatch between multi-modal and baseline models. These are all substantive concerns that affect the validity of the comparison.
round_transcripts.defender: All three remain confirmed as unresolved design gaps.

all_issues_adjudicated: Test set of ~100 patients too small for statistically distinguishable AUROC comparison between multi-modal and genomic-only models
Random split may confound treatment era and protocol changes over 5-year cohort; temporal sensitivity analysis not described
Multi-modal model has more parameters than genomic-only baseline; capacity mismatch uncontrolled
